pendulum-exercise.name: Pendulum Exercise
pendulum-exercise.description: An exercise that reduces tension and improves shoulder range of motion using gravity assistance. Ideal for the early stages of rehabilitation.
steps: Stand beside a table, lean slightly forward, and support yourself with the unaffected hand on the table
Let the affected arm hang straight down
Swing the arm in small counterclockwise circles 10 times
Swing the arm in small clockwise circles 10 times
Swing the arm forward and back 10 times, then side to side 10 times
Perform 2–3 sets, 2–3 times per day
cautions: Do not use shoulder muscles to swing — let the arm's weight do the work
Stop immediately if pain becomes severe
Avoid during acute inflammation phases

external-rotation-band.name: External Rotation with Resistance Band
external-rotation-band.description: Strengthens the rotator cuff, particularly the infraspinatus and teres minor, improving shoulder stability.
steps: Stand or sit with elbow bent 90 degrees and tucked against your side
Hold the resistance band with the exercising hand; anchor the other end to a door or post
Slowly rotate the arm outward, keeping the elbow against your side
Hold for 2 seconds, then slowly return to the starting position
Perform 15 repetitions for 3 sets
cautions: Keep elbow against your body throughout the movement
Rotate slowly — no jerking
Use a band resistance that does not cause pain

shoulder-blade-squeeze.name: Shoulder Blade Squeeze (Scapular Retraction)
shoulder-blade-squeeze.description: Strengthens the muscles around the shoulder blades to support the shoulder joint and reduce re-injury risk.
steps: Sit or stand with an upright posture
Draw your shoulders back to squeeze the shoulder blades together
Hold for 5 seconds
Relax
Perform 10–15 repetitions for 3 sets
cautions: Do not shrug the shoulders upward — keep them at a neutral level
Breathe normally throughout the exercise

towel-stretch.name: Towel Stretch
towel-stretch.description: Improves internal rotation range of motion in the shoulder. Suitable for all stages of frozen shoulder.
steps: Hold a towel with both hands behind your back
Good hand on top, affected hand on the bottom
Slowly pull the towel up with the good hand to lift the affected hand
Hold the stretch for 15–30 seconds
Perform 3–5 repetitions, 3 rounds per day
cautions: You may feel a stretch but should not have significant pain
Pull slowly — no jerking

wall-climbing.name: Wall Climbing / Finger Walk
wall-climbing.description: Gradually increases arm elevation range of motion and reduces pain-related fear during movement.
steps: Stand facing the wall at arm's length distance
Place your fingertips on the wall at waist level
Slowly walk your fingers up the wall until you feel a gentle stretch
Hold for 5 seconds, then slowly walk fingers back down
Perform 10–20 repetitions, 2–3 rounds per day
cautions: Stand close enough so you don't fully extend your arm at the start
Move slowly — no rushing
Stop and rest if pain becomes severe

cross-body-stretch.name: Cross-Body Shoulder Stretch
cross-body-stretch.description: Stretches the posterior shoulder capsule to improve internal rotation range of motion.
steps: Sit or stand upright
Raise the affected arm to shoulder level
Use the good hand to grasp the elbow of the affected arm
Gently pull the arm across the chest
Hold for 20–30 seconds
Perform 3–5 repetitions, 3 rounds per day
cautions: Do not pull too hard — a gentle stretch is sufficient
Avoid shrugging the shoulder during the stretch

cat-cow-stretch.name: Cat-Cow Stretch
cat-cow-stretch.description: Improves spinal flexibility, reduces back muscle tension, and promotes circulation in the intervertebral discs.
steps: Get on all fours with hands under shoulders and knees under hips
Inhale: arch the back down, lift the head and tailbone (Cow position)
Exhale: round the back upward, tuck the head and pelvis (Cat position)
Alternate slowly in sync with your breathing
Perform 10–15 repetitions for 3 sets
cautions: Move slowly in sync with breathing — don't force it
Stop if you feel sharp pain or pain radiating down the leg
Avoid during acute severe flare-ups

childs-pose.name: Child's Pose
childs-pose.description: Stretches the lower back, hips, and thighs, reducing pressure on the spine.
steps: Kneel and sit back on your heels
Slowly lean forward, extending arms in front or alongside the body
Rest your forehead on the floor (or as close as possible)
Breathe deeply and relax the back further with each exhale
Hold for 30 seconds to 2 minutes
cautions: Place a pillow under the knees if you have knee problems
Not suitable if you have pain radiating down the leg

bird-dog.name: Bird Dog
bird-dog.description: Strengthens the core muscles and back, improving spinal stability.
steps: Get on all fours with hands and knees on the floor
Simultaneously extend the right arm forward and left leg backward
Hold for 5–10 seconds with the body parallel to the floor
Slowly return to the starting position, then switch sides
Perform 10 repetitions per side for 3 sets
cautions: Do not twist the torso or lift the hips
Look down at the floor — do not raise the head
Start from the all-fours position if not yet strong enough

wrist-extension-stretch.name: Wrist Extension Stretch
wrist-extension-stretch.description: Stretches the muscles and tendons on the outer elbow, reducing tension in the extensor tendon at the lateral epicondyle.
steps: Extend the affected arm in front of you with the elbow straight
Use the other hand to gently press the palm downward until you feel a stretch on the outer forearm
Hold for 15–30 seconds
Slowly release
Perform 3–5 repetitions, 3–4 rounds per day
cautions: Keep the elbow straight — do not bend it
Stretch to a point of tension, not pain
Perform slowly and consistently

eccentric-wrist-extension.name: Eccentric Wrist Extension (Tyler Twist)
eccentric-wrist-extension.description: Eccentric loading helps treat chronic tendon issues by stimulating tendon repair.
steps: Hold a light resistance band or weight in the affected hand with the palm facing up
Use the good hand to assist lifting the wrist upward (concentric phase)
Release the good hand, then slowly lower the wrist using the affected hand alone (eccentric phase — approximately 3 seconds)
Perform 15 repetitions for 3 sets, once daily
cautions: Start with minimal resistance
Mild discomfort during the exercise is normal, but stop if pain is significant
Perform under physiotherapist supervision initially

gentle-walking.name: Gentle Walking Program
gentle-walking.description: Walking is a safe aerobic exercise for fibromyalgia that helps reduce pain, fatigue, and improve mood.
steps: Start with 5–10 minutes of walking per day at a moderate pace
Gradually increase by 2–5 minutes per week
Goal: 30 minutes, 5 times per week
Walk on flat, well-ventilated surfaces
Listen to your body — rest if you feel excessively fatigued
cautions: Avoid overexertion in the early days (post-exertional malaise)
Reduce duration if pain increases after walking
Walk with a companion or inform someone at home before going out

gentle-neck-stretch.name: Gentle Neck Stretches
gentle-neck-stretch.description: Reduces tension in the neck and shoulder muscles, which are common pain points for fibromyalgia patients.
steps: Sit or stand upright with relaxed shoulders
Tilt the head to the right, bringing the ear toward the right shoulder — hold 15–20 seconds
Slowly return and repeat on the left side
Slowly lower the chin to the chest — hold for 15 seconds
Return to neutral; perform 3–5 repetitions per direction
cautions: Do not roll the neck in full circles — may cause dizziness
Move slowly — no jerking
Stop immediately if you feel numbness or pain radiating down the arm

calf-stretch.name: Gastrocnemius / Soleus Calf Stretch
calf-stretch.description: Stretches the calf muscles and Achilles tendon, which are often tight in plantar fasciitis patients, reducing tension on the plantar fascia.
steps: Stand facing a wall with hands placed on the wall
Step the leg to be stretched backward, with the front knee slightly bent
Press the back heel firmly into the floor, keeping the back knee straight — feel the stretch in the calf
Hold for 30 seconds
Slightly bend the back knee to stretch the soleus — hold for another 30 seconds
Perform 3 repetitions per leg, 2–3 rounds per day
cautions: Keep the heel on the floor throughout — do not lift it
Stand on a flat, non-slip surface

plantar-fascia-stretch.name: Plantar Fascia Stretch (Seated Towel Stretch)
plantar-fascia-stretch.description: Directly stretches the plantar fascia. Most effective when performed before taking the first steps in the morning.
steps: Sit in a chair and cross the affected foot over the opposite knee
Use your hand to grasp all the toes and gently pull them toward you
Use the other hand to gently massage the underside of the foot
Hold for 15–30 seconds
Perform 3 repetitions every morning before getting out of bed
cautions: Pull the toes until you feel a stretch under the foot — not to the point of pain
Perform consistently every morning for best results

toe-curls.name: Toe Curls with Towel
toe-curls.description: Strengthens the intrinsic foot muscles to support the plantar fascia and reduce inflammation long-term.
steps: Sit in a chair and place a small towel flat on the floor
Place your foot on the towel
Use your toes to scrunch the towel toward you
Perform 10–15 repetitions for 3 sets
cautions: Perform slowly with controlled movement
Rest if you experience cramping
